lines.translation: Hello, may I ask if Miss Li You is there?
This is she. May I ask who you are?
My last name is Fei. Do you still remember me?
Do you still remember Gao Xiaoyin's birthday party last month? I'm the person who asked you to dance at the end. Think again. Do you remember now?
I'm Gao Xiaoyin's middle school classmate.
Really? How did you know my phone number?
Mr. Fei, is there something you need?
Are you free this weekend? I'd like to ask you to go dancing.
This weekend won't work. I have three tests next week.
No problem, how about next weekend? After you finish your tests, we can go have a good time.
Next weekend won't work either. I have to move out of the dorm, so I have to clean and tidy up the room.
How about the weekend after next, is that okay?
Sorry, the weekend after next is even worse. I'm going to travel to New York with my boyfriend.
Mr. Fei, sorry, my phone is out of battery. Goodbye!

language_notes.label: a
language_notes.explanation_en: While 记得 (jìde) pertains to the continuous state of remembering, 想起来 (xiǎng qi lai) refers to the mental act of retrieving information from your memory. Thus you can say: 我记得他上过我的课, 可是我想不起来他叫什么名字。(Wǒ jìde tā shàng guo wǒ de kè, kěshì wǒ xiǎng bu qi lai tā jiào shénme míngzi) (I remember that he took my class, but I can't recall his name at the moment).

language_notes.label: b
language_notes.explanation_en: This colloquial expression means "all out, to one's heart's content" and often precedes a verb to serve as an adverbial, e.g.: 考试以后我要去纽约好好儿玩儿玩儿 (Kǎo shì yìhòu wǒ yào qù Niǔyuē hǎohāor wánr wanr) (After the test I want to go to New York and have a great time). Note the different tone for the reduplicated syllable 好 (hǎo).

grammar_sections.title: Directional complements (II)
grammar_sections.explanation_en:
Directional complements indicate the direction in which a person or an object moves. A directional verb such as 上 (shang) (to go up), 下 (xia) (to go down), 进 (jin) (to go in), 出 (chu) (to go out), 回 (hui) (to return), 过 (guo) (to go over, to pass), 起 (qi) (to rise), 开 (kai) (to part from), 到 (dao) (to arrive), 来 (lai) (to come), and 去 (qu) (to go) can be placed after another verb to become what is known as a "simple directional complement."
When a simple directional complement—上/下/进/出/回/过/起/到 (shang/xia/jin/chu/hui/guo/qi/dao)—is combined with 来 (lai) or 去 (qu), we have what is called a "compound directional complement," again with two basic patterns.

patterns.pattern:
Simple directional complements: Pattern A1
Subject + verb + place word/noun (phrase) + 来/去

Simple directional complements: Pattern A2
Subject + verb + 来/去 + noun

Simple directional complements: Pattern B
Subject + verb + 上/下/进/出/回/过/起/开/到 + place word/noun

Compound directional complements: Pattern A
Subject + verb + 上/下/进/出/回/过/起/到 + place word/noun + 来/去
patterns.pattern_pinyin: Subject + verb + shang/xia/jin/chu/hui/guo/qi/dao + place word/noun + lai/qu

patterns.pattern:
Compound directional complements: Pattern B
Subject + verb + 上/下/进/出/回/过/起 + 来/去 + noun

usage_notes: Simple directional complements: When the object of the verb is a location word as in (A) and (B), the sentence can only appear in Pattern A1. When the object is a regular noun and the action is not completed, the sentence often appears in Pattern A1 as well, as in (C) and (D). If the action is completed, the sentence can appear either in Pattern A1 or in Pattern A2. It is, therefore, a good idea to memorize Pattern A1 as the failsafe form.

Simple directional complements: In Pattern B, note that when the directional complement is 上/下/进/出/回/过/起/开/到, regardless if the object is a place word or an ordinary noun, if the action is completed or not, the object comes after the directional complement.

Compound directional complements: As in the case of the simple directional compounds, when the object is a location word, the sentence appears only in Pattern A, as in (L), (M), and (N). If the object is a regular noun and the action is not completed, the sentence often appears in Pattern A as well, as in (O) and (P). If the action is completed, the sentence can appear either in Pattern A or in Pattern B as in (Q), (R), and (S). Again, it is not a bad idea to memorize Pattern A as the failsafe form.

scenes.scene_description_cn: 傍晚，李友在舒适的公寓书房里看书，手机响起。她接起电话。画面切为分屏，左边是李友，右边是来电者费先生。费先生礼貌地询问，李友确认身份后，带着一丝困惑反问对方。
scenes.setting_en: Split screen. Left: Li You's cozy, modern apartment study room at dusk. Right: A dark, non-descript space for Mr. Fei.
scenes.video_prompt_en: Split screen video, cinematic realism. On the left, a young Asian woman (Li You) in a cozy sweater sits in her apartment study, she answers her phone with a neutral expression. On the right, a young Asian man (Mr. Fei) smiles warmly into his phone, his background is dark and out of focus. Her expression turns to curiosity as she listens and then speaks.

segments.teaching_goal: Learn how to start a phone call and ask if someone is available.

narration.subtitle_en: The conversation starts with a phone call. The first word is [zh:喂], which is the standard way to say 'hello' on the phone in Chinese. Then, the speaker uses [zh:请问], a polite phrase meaning 'may I ask'. He's looking for [zh:李友小姐]. [zh:小姐] means 'Miss', and it's used after the surname or full name. Finally, [zh:在吗] is a common way to ask if someone is present or available. The whole sentence means 'Hello, may I ask if Miss Li You is there?'

on_screen_text.focus_text: 喂,请问李友小姐在吗?
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: wèi, qǐngwèn Lǐ Yǒu xiǎojiě zài ma?
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Hello, may I ask if Miss Li You is there?

segments.visual_notes: Animate a phone icon ringing. Highlight each keyword as it's explained.

segments.teaching_goal: Learn how to confirm your identity and ask for the caller's name politely.

narration.subtitle_en: Li You confirms her identity by saying [zh:我就是]. This is a very common and standard way to say 'This is she' or 'Speaking' on the phone. The word [zh:就] adds emphasis here. She then asks who is calling with [zh:请问你是哪一位]. Notice she uses [zh:哪一位] instead of 'shéi'. [zh:位] is a polite measure word for people, so [zh:哪一位] is a more formal and polite way to ask 'who are you?'

on_screen_text.focus_text: 我就是。请问你是哪一位?
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: wǒ jiùshì. qǐngwèn nǐ shì nǎ yí wèi?
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: This is she. May I ask who you are?

segments.visual_notes: Show a profile icon for Li You. When '位' is mentioned, show it next to a person icon with a 'polite' label.

segments.teaching_goal: Learn to state your surname and ask if someone remembers you.

narration.subtitle_en: Mr. Fei introduces himself with [zh:我姓费], 'My surname is Fei'. He then asks, [zh:你还记得我吗]. The key verb here is [zh:记得], which means 'to remember'. The adverb [zh:还] means 'still', so the question is 'Do you still remember me?'. This is a common question when you're not sure if someone recalls who you are. We'll explore the word [zh:记得] more in a bit.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 我姓费,你还记得我吗?
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: wǒ xìng Fèi, nǐ hái jìde wǒ ma?
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: My last name is Fei. Do you still remember me?

segments.visual_notes: Highlight '姓' and show a name card. Highlight '记得' and show a thought bubble icon.

segments.teaching_goal: Understand how repeating a word can form a question showing confusion.

narration.subtitle_en: Li You responds with a short, questioning phrase: [zh:姓费]. She is repeating what he said to confirm, and also to show that she is trying to remember. This is a very natural conversational technique in Chinese. By repeating a key piece of information with a rising intonation, you can show you're processing it or that you're not quite sure.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 姓费?
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: xìng Fèi?
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Last name Fei?

segments.visual_notes: Show a question mark icon appearing next to the text.

segments.teaching_goal: Learn phrases to help someone remember a past event and introduce 'xiǎng qi lai'.

narration.subtitle_en: Mr. Fei tries to jog Li You's memory. He asks if she remembers the [zh:生日舞会], or 'birthday dance party', from [zh:上个月], 'last month'. He gives a big clue: [zh:我就是最后请你跳舞的那个人], 'I am the person who invited you to dance at the end.' He then says [zh:你再想想], 'Think again.' Finally, he asks [zh:想起来了吗], 'Do you remember now?'. This introduces a key phrase, [zh:想起来], which means 'to recall' or 'to bring to mind'.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 你还记得...吗? ...你再想想。想起来了吗?
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: nǐ hái jìde...ma? ...nǐ zài xiǎngxiang. xiǎng qǐlai le ma?
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Do you still remember...? ...Think again. Do you remember now?
on_screen_text.notes: A long sentence broken down for clarity.

segments.visual_notes: Use icons for a calendar (last month), a birthday cake (birthday), and dancing people (dance party).

segments.segment_title: Usage: 记得 vs. 想起来
segments.teaching_goal: Distinguish between 'jìde' (the state of remembering) and 'xiǎng qi lai' (the action of recalling).

narration.subtitle_en: Let's clarify the difference between [zh:记得] and [zh:想起来]. [zh:记得] refers to the continuous state of remembering something, like having a memory stored in your brain. [zh:想起来], on the other hand, is the action of successfully retrieving that memory from your brain at a specific moment. For example, you could say 'I remember that he took my class, but I can't recall his name right now.' In Chinese, that would be: [zh:我记得他上过我的课，可是我想不起来他叫什么名字]. So, [zh:记得] is the state, and [zh:想起来] is the action.

highlight_words.explanation_en: Refers to the continuous state of having a memory.
Refers to the mental act of retrieving a memory.

segments.teaching_goal: Learn the negative form 'xiǎng bu qǐlai' to say you can't remember.

narration.subtitle_en: Li You's response is [zh:对不起,我想不起来]. She starts with [zh:对不起], 'sorry'. Then she says [zh:我想不起来]. This is the negative form of the phrase we just learned. The structure 'Verb + bu + Complement' is used to negate potential complements. So, [zh:想起来] means 'to be able to recall', and [zh:想不起来] means 'to be unable to recall'. It's a polite way of saying 'I can't remember'.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 对不起,我想不起来。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: duìbuqǐ, wǒ xiǎng bu qǐlai.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Sorry, I can't recall.

segments.visual_notes: Show the structure '想 + 不 + 起来' to emphasize the negative form.

segments.teaching_goal: Learn vocabulary for school connections.

narration.subtitle_en: Mr. Fei provides another piece of information. He says, [zh:我是高小音的中学同学]. This means, 'I am Gao Xiaoyin's middle school classmate.' The word [zh:中学] means 'middle school', and [zh:同学] means 'classmate'. This is a common way to establish a connection through a mutual friend.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 我是高小音的中学同学。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: wǒ shì Gāo Xiǎoyīn de zhōngxué tóngxué.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: I'm Gao Xiaoyin's middle school classmate.

segments.visual_notes: Show an icon of a school, then two stick figures labeled '同学'.

segments.teaching_goal: Learn to ask 'how' something happened using the 'shì...de' structure.

narration.subtitle_en: Li You is surprised. She says [zh:是吗], 'Really?'. Then she asks, [zh:你是怎么知道我的电话号码的]. This is a very useful sentence pattern: 'shì...de' is used to ask for details about a past event, in this case, 'how' he got her number. [zh:怎么] means 'how', [zh:知道] means 'to know', and [zh:电话号码] is 'phone number'. The question emphasizes the manner in which the action took place.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 是吗?你是怎么知道我的电话号码的?
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: shì ma? nǐ shì zěnme zhīdào wǒ de diànhuà hàomǎ de?
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Really? How did you know my phone number?
on_screen_text.notes: The '是...的' (shì...de) structure emphasizes details of a past action.

segments.visual_notes: Highlight the '是...的' structure around the main verb phrase.

segments.teaching_goal: Learn how to answer a 'shì...de' question.

narration.subtitle_en: Mr. Fei's answer uses the same 'shì...de' structure. He says, [zh:是小音告诉我的]. This means, 'It was Xiaoyin who told me.' The word [zh:告诉] means 'to tell'. By using this structure, he is directly answering the 'how' or 'who' aspect of her question, emphasizing that Xiaoyin was the source of the information.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 是小音告诉我的。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: shì Xiǎoyīn gàosu wǒ de.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Xiaoyin told me.

segments.visual_notes: Show an arrow from 'Xiaoyin' to the phone number, illustrating the act of 'telling'.

segments.teaching_goal: Learn the phrase 'yǒu shì ma' to ask about the purpose of a call.

narration.subtitle_en: Li You gets to the point. She says, [zh:费先生,你有事吗]. She addresses him as [zh:费先生], 'Mr. Fei'. The question [zh:你有事吗] literally means 'Do you have business/matter?'. In this context, it's a way of asking 'Is there something you need?' or 'What's up?'. It can sound a bit direct, suggesting she wants to know the purpose of his call.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 费先生,你有事吗?
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Fèi xiānsheng, nǐ yǒu shì ma?
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Mr. Fei, is there something you need?

highlight_words.translation: to be occupied; to have something to do

segments.visual_notes: Show a speech bubble with a question mark inside, indicating 'what's the purpose?'.

segments.teaching_goal: Learn how to ask if someone is free and invite them out.

narration.subtitle_en: Here comes the invitation. Mr. Fei asks, [zh:这个周末你有空儿吗]. [zh:这个周末] is 'this weekend', and [zh:有空儿] means 'to have free time'. Then he states his intention: [zh:我想请你去跳舞]. [zh:想] means 'would like to', and [zh:请] means 'to invite'. So, he's saying, 'I'd like to invite you to go dancing.' This is a classic way to ask someone out.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 这个周末你有空儿吗?我想请你去跳舞。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: zhè ge zhōumò nǐ yǒu kòngr ma? wǒ xiǎng qǐng nǐ qù tiàowǔ.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Are you free this weekend? I'd like to ask you to go dancing.

highlight_words.translation: to want to; would like to

segments.visual_notes: Show a calendar icon with the weekend highlighted. Then an icon of two people dancing.

segments.teaching_goal: Learn how to refuse an invitation and give a reason.

narration.subtitle_en: Here is the first refusal. Li You says, [zh:这个周末不行]. The word [zh:不行] means 'won't work' or 'not okay', and it's a common way to decline. She then gives a reason: [zh:下个星期我有三个考试]. [zh:下个星期] is 'next week', and [zh:考试] means 'test' or 'exam'. Having three exams is a very solid reason to be busy!

on_screen_text.focus_text: 这个周末不行,下个星期我有三个考试。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: zhè ge zhōumò bù xíng, xià ge xīngqī wǒ yǒu sān ge kǎoshì.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: This weekend won't work. I have three tests next week.

segments.visual_notes: Show a big red 'X' over the weekend on the calendar. Then show icons of three test papers.

segments.teaching_goal: Learn phrases for making an alternative suggestion.

narration.subtitle_en: Mr. Fei doesn't give up. He starts with [zh:没关系], 'no problem'. He then suggests an alternative: [zh:下个周末怎么样], 'How about next weekend?'. He adds an incentive: [zh:你考完试,我们好好儿玩儿玩儿]. [zh:考完试] means 'after you finish your tests'. The phrase [zh:好好儿玩儿玩儿] means 'we can have a really good time'. We'll look at this special expression next.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 没关系,下个周末怎么样? ...我们好好儿玩儿玩儿。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: méi guānxi, xià ge zhōumò zěnme yàng? ...wǒmen hǎohāor wánr wánr.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: No problem, how about next weekend? ...we can go have a good time.

segments.visual_notes: Show a checkmark on 'next weekend' on the calendar. Animate the words '好好儿' to sparkle.

segments.teaching_goal: Understand the colloquial expression 'hǎohāor' to mean 'all out' or 'to one's heart's content'.

narration.subtitle_en: Let's focus on the expression [zh:好好儿]. This is a colloquial phrase that means 'all out' or 'to one's heart's content'. It often comes before a verb to act as an adverbial, emphasizing that the action should be done thoroughly and enjoyably. For example, [zh:好好儿学习] means 'study hard', and [zh:好好儿休息] means 'get a good rest'. In the dialogue, [zh:好好儿玩儿玩儿] means 'to have a really great time'. Note the tone on the second 'hao' is neutral or light, not the third tone.

highlight_words.translation: all out; to one's heart's content
highlight_words.explanation_en: A colloquial expression used as an adverbial to emphasize doing an action thoroughly or with great enjoyment.

segments.teaching_goal: Learn vocabulary related to moving and cleaning.

narration.subtitle_en: Li You refuses again. She says, [zh:下个周末也不行], 'Next weekend also won't work.' Her reason this time is: [zh:我要从宿舍搬出去]. This means 'I have to move out of the dorm'. [zh:搬] is 'to move', and [zh:出去] means 'out'. She adds, [zh:得打扫、整理房间]. The word [zh:得] means 'must'. The two verbs are [zh:打扫], 'to clean', and [zh:整理], 'to tidy up' the [zh:房间], or 'room'.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 下个周末也不行,我要从宿舍搬出去,得打扫、整理房间。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: xià ge zhōumò yě bù xíng, wǒ yào cóng sùshè bān chūqù, děi dǎsǎo, zhěnglǐ fángjiān.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Next weekend won't work either. I have to move out of the dorm, so I have to clean and tidy up the room.

segments.visual_notes: Show an icon of a person carrying a box out of a building. Then show icons for a broom (打扫) and a neat stack of books (整理).

segments.teaching_goal: Learn how to say 'the weekend after next' and a soft way to make a suggestion.

narration.subtitle_en: Amazingly, Mr. Fei tries a third time! He says, [zh:你看下下个周末,好不好]. The phrase [zh:下下个周末] means 'the weekend after next'. The structure [zh:你看..., 好不好] is a very soft and suggestive way to propose an idea. It's like saying, 'How about you consider..., is that okay?'. It's less direct than just asking a question.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 你看下下个周末,好不好?
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: nǐ kàn xià xià ge zhōumò, hǎo bu hǎo?
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: How about the weekend after next, is that okay?

segments.visual_notes: Show a calendar flipping two weeks ahead. Highlight the '好不好' structure.

segments.teaching_goal: Learn how to give a final, undeniable reason for refusal.

narration.subtitle_en: This is the final, decisive refusal. Li You says [zh:下下个周末更不行了]. The word [zh:更] means 'even more', so this means 'The weekend after next is even less possible!'. She delivers the final blow with her reason: [zh:我要跟我的男朋友去纽约旅行]. 'I'm going to travel to New York with my boyfriend.' This introduces [zh:男朋友] for 'boyfriend' and [zh:旅行] for 'to travel'. This is a very clear and unambiguous excuse.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 对不起,下下个周末更不行了,我要跟我的男朋友去纽约旅行。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: duìbuqǐ, xià xià ge zhōumò gèng bùxíng le, wǒ yào gēn wǒ de nánpéngyou qù Niǔyuē lǚxíng.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Sorry, the weekend after next is even worse. I'm going to travel to New York with my boyfriend.

segments.visual_notes: Show an icon of a couple holding hands, next to a landmark for New York like the Statue of Liberty.

segments.teaching_goal: Recognize filler words that indicate hesitation.

narration.subtitle_en: After hearing about the boyfriend, Mr. Fei is speechless. He just says [zh:那]. In this context, with the pauses, [zh:那] is a filler word that shows hesitation or that he's at a loss for what to say next. It's like saying 'Oh... well...' in English. He has finally run out of suggestions.

on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: ...Oh... well...

segments.visual_notes: Show ellipsis points '...' growing on screen to represent the awkward pause.

segments.teaching_goal: Learn a common excuse to end a phone call abruptly.

narration.subtitle_en: Li You quickly ends the awkward conversation. She uses another excuse: [zh:我的手机没电了], 'My phone is out of battery.' This is a very common, if not always true, way to get off the phone quickly. She then says a quick [zh:再见], 'goodbye', and hangs up. This is a very clear signal that the conversation is over.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 费先生,对不起,我的手机没电了。再见!
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: Fèi xiānsheng, duìbuqǐ, wǒ de shǒujī méi diàn le. zàijiàn!
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Mr. Fei, sorry, my phone is out of battery. Goodbye!

segments.visual_notes: Show a phone icon with a battery level that quickly drops to zero.

segments.teaching_goal: Understand the context of a final, unanswered 'wèi'.

narration.subtitle_en: The dialogue ends with Mr. Fei left on the line. He says [zh:喂] again, but this time it's not a greeting. He's trying to see if she's still there, like saying 'Hello? Hello?' into a dead line. The long pauses indicated by the ellipsis show his confusion and the abrupt end of the call. Poor Mr. Fei!

on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: wèi... wèi...
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Hello...? Hello...?

segments.visual_notes: Show the word '喂' on screen, then have it fade out, followed by a 'call ended' screen.

segments.teaching_goal: To understand simple directional complements using verbs like 上, 下, 进, 出 combined with 来 or 去.

narration.subtitle_en: Let's dive into a key grammar point: directional complements. These show the direction of an action. A simple directional complement is formed by a verb plus a direction word like [zh:上], [zh:下], [zh:进], or [zh:出]. To specify direction relative to the speaker, we add [zh:来] for 'coming' or [zh:去] for 'going'. For example, in the dialogue, Li You says she has to [zh:搬出去], 'move out'. The action is [zh:搬], 'to move', and the direction is [zh:出去], 'out and away from' where she currently is. The choice between [zh:来] and [zh:去] depends entirely on the speaker's perspective.

on_screen_text.focus_text: Verb + [上/下/进/出] + [来/去]
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Structure for Simple Directional Complements
on_screen_text.notes: 来 (lái) indicates movement towards the speaker, while 去 (qù) indicates movement away.

grammar_points.pattern_pinyin: wǒ yào cóng sùshè bān chūqù
grammar_points.explanation_en: I need to move out of the dorm. (The speaker is inside, moving away).
She is coming downstairs. (The speaker is downstairs).
The teacher is walking into the classroom. (The speaker is outside the classroom).

segments.visual_notes: Animate an arrow moving away from a dorm for '搬出去', an arrow coming down stairs for '下楼来', and an arrow going into a classroom for '走进教室去'.

segments.teaching_goal: To understand compound and abstract uses of directional complements, like 想起来.

narration.subtitle_en: Directional complements can also be abstract. The dialogue gives us a fantastic example with [zh:想起来]. Here, [zh:想] means 'to think' and the complement [zh:起来] implies an upward or emerging action. Literally 'think up,' it means 'to recall' or 'to bring a memory to the surface.' When Li You says [zh:我想不起来], she uses the potential form by inserting [zh:不] between the verb and the complement. This shows the action of recalling is not achievable at that moment. This is a very common and useful pattern for expressing ability or inability.

on_screen_text.focus_text: Q: 想起来了吗？ A: 我想不起来。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: xiǎng qǐlai le ma? wǒ xiǎng bu qǐlai.
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Q: Do you remember now? A: I can't recall.
on_screen_text.notes: The potential form: Verb + 得/不 + Complement

grammar_points.explanation_en: Affirmative: 'I recalled it.' The action was successfully completed.
Negative Potential: 'I cannot recall.' The action is not achievable.

grammar_points.pattern_pinyin: tā mǎi huí lai le yì xiē shuǐguǒ
grammar_points.explanation_en: Compound complement: He bought some fruit and brought it back here. (Action: buy; Direction: back here).

segments.visual_notes: Show a lightbulb icon appearing over a character's head for '想起来'. For '想不起来', show the lightbulb flickering and disappearing.

segments.segment_title: Usage: 记得 vs. 想起来
segments.teaching_goal: To distinguish between the state of remembering (记得) and the action of recalling (想起来).

narration.subtitle_en: Let's clarify the difference between two words for 'remember': [zh:记得] and [zh:想起来]. [zh:记得] describes the continuous state of having a memory. In the dialogue, Mr. Fei asks, [zh:你还记得我吗], 'Do you still remember me?' He's asking if the memory of him exists in her mind. On the other hand, [zh:想起来] is the action of successfully retrieving a memory. When Mr. Fei asks [zh:想起来了吗], he's asking, 'Have you managed to recall it now?' Li You's reply, [zh:我想不起来], means she is trying, but cannot complete the action of recalling. So, [zh:记得] is about the state, while [zh:想起来] is about the action.

highlight_words.explanation_en: Refers to the continuous state of having something in one's memory.
Refers to the action of retrieving a memory, often after some thought.

segments.visual_notes: Show two columns. Left column for '记得' with an icon of a brain with information stored. Right column for '想起来' with an icon of a file being retrieved from a cabinet.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn the colloquial adverb 好好儿, meaning 'thoroughly' or 'to one's heart's content'.

narration.subtitle_en: Now let's look at the colloquial expression [zh:好好儿]. When Mr. Fei says [zh:我们好好儿玩儿玩儿], he's suggesting they 'have a really good time' or 'enjoy themselves to the fullest.' [zh:好好儿] functions as an adverb, placed before a verb, to indicate that an action should be done thoroughly, properly, or with great enjoyment. Notice the tone on the second [zh:好] is neutral, so we say 'hǎohāor'. You can use it for many activities, like [zh:好好儿学习] 'study hard' or [zh:好好儿休息] 'get a good rest'.

on_screen_text.focus_text: 我们好好儿玩儿玩儿。
on_screen_text.focus_pinyin: wǒmen hǎohāor wánr wánr
on_screen_text.focus_gloss_en: Let's go have a good time.
on_screen_text.notes: 好好儿 (hǎohāor) often precedes a reduplicated verb to add emphasis.

highlight_words.translation: all out, to one's heart's content
highlight_words.explanation_en: A colloquial adverb used to express doing an action thoroughly or with great enjoyment.

grammar_points.explanation_en: Structure: Use 'hǎohāor' before a verb to intensify it.
Example: You must prepare for the test thoroughly.

segments.visual_notes: Animate the characters '好好儿' glowing before the verb '玩儿玩儿' to show its adverbial function.

segments.teaching_goal: To review the key vocabulary and grammar patterns for inviting and refusing invitations.

narration.subtitle_en: Great job! Let's quickly review the key points from this lesson. We learned how to use directional complements like [zh:搬出去] and [zh:想起来], and how to differentiate between [zh:记得] and [zh:想起来]. We also covered the useful expression [zh:好好儿] for doing something to the fullest. Keep practicing these phrases for inviting and refusing!

segments.teaching_goal: Learn how to start a phone call and ask if someone is available.

narration_track.subtitle_en: The conversation starts with a phone call. The first word is [zh:喂], which is the standard way to say 'hello' on the phone in Chinese. Then, the speaker uses [zh:请问], a polite phrase meaning 'may I ask'. He's looking for [zh:李友小姐]. [zh:小姐] means 'Miss', and it's used after the surname or full name. Finally, [zh:在吗] is a common way to ask if someone is present or available. The whole sentence means 'Hello, may I ask if Miss Li You is there?'

content.focus_text: 喂,请问李友小姐在吗?
content.focus_pinyin: wèi, qǐngwèn Lǐ Yǒu xiǎojiě zài ma?
content.focus_gloss_en: Hello, may I ask if Miss Li You is there?

segments.visual_notes: Animate a phone icon ringing. Highlight each keyword as it's explained.

segments.teaching_goal: Learn how to confirm your identity and ask for the caller's name politely.

narration_track.subtitle_en: Li You confirms her identity by saying [zh:我就是]. This is a very common and standard way to say 'This is she' or 'Speaking' on the phone. The word [zh:就] adds emphasis here. She then asks who is calling with [zh:请问你是哪一位]. Notice she uses [zh:哪一位] instead of 'shéi'. [zh:位] is a polite measure word for people, so [zh:哪一位] is a more formal and polite way to ask 'who are you?'

content.focus_text: 我就是。请问你是哪一位?
content.focus_pinyin: wǒ jiùshì. qǐngwèn nǐ shì nǎ yí wèi?
content.focus_gloss_en: This is she. May I ask who you are?

segments.visual_notes: Show a profile icon for Li You. When '位' is mentioned, show it next to a person icon with a 'polite' label.

segments.teaching_goal: Learn to state your surname and ask if someone remembers you.

narration_track.subtitle_en: Mr. Fei introduces himself with [zh:我姓费], 'My surname is Fei'. He then asks, [zh:你还记得我吗]. The key verb here is [zh:记得], which means 'to remember'. The adverb [zh:还] means 'still', so the question is 'Do you still remember me?'. This is a common question when you're not sure if someone recalls who you are. We'll explore the word [zh:记得] more in a bit.

content.focus_text: 我姓费,你还记得我吗?
content.focus_pinyin: wǒ xìng Fèi, nǐ hái jìde wǒ ma?
content.focus_gloss_en: My last name is Fei. Do you still remember me?

segments.visual_notes: Highlight '姓' and show a name card. Highlight '记得' and show a thought bubble icon.

segments.teaching_goal: Understand how repeating a word can form a question showing confusion.

narration_track.subtitle_en: Li You responds with a short, questioning phrase: [zh:姓费]. She is repeating what he said to confirm, and also to show that she is trying to remember. This is a very natural conversational technique in Chinese. By repeating a key piece of information with a rising intonation, you can show you're processing it or that you're not quite sure.

content.focus_text: 姓费?
content.focus_pinyin: xìng Fèi?
content.focus_gloss_en: Last name Fei?

segments.visual_notes: Show a question mark icon appearing next to the text.

segments.teaching_goal: Learn phrases to help someone remember a past event and introduce 'xiǎng qi lai'.

narration_track.subtitle_en: Mr. Fei tries to jog Li You's memory. He asks if she remembers the [zh:生日舞会], or 'birthday dance party', from [zh:上个月], 'last month'. He gives a big clue: [zh:我就是最后请你跳舞的那个人], 'I am the person who invited you to dance at the end.' He then says [zh:你再想想], 'Think again.' Finally, he asks [zh:想起来了吗], 'Do you remember now?'. This introduces a key phrase, [zh:想起来], which means 'to recall' or 'to bring to mind'.

content.focus_text: 你还记得...吗? ...你再想想。想起来了吗?
content.focus_pinyin: nǐ hái jìde...ma? ...nǐ zài xiǎngxiang. xiǎng qǐlai le ma?
content.focus_gloss_en: Do you still remember...? ...Think again. Do you remember now?

content.notes: A long sentence broken down for clarity.

segments.visual_notes: Use icons for a calendar (last month), a birthday cake (birthday), and dancing people (dance party).

segments.segment_title: Usage: 记得 vs. 想起来
segments.teaching_goal: Distinguish between 'jìde' (the state of remembering) and 'xiǎng qi lai' (the action of recalling).

narration_track.subtitle_en: Let's clarify the difference between [zh:记得] and [zh:想起来]. [zh:记得] refers to the continuous state of remembering something, like having a memory stored in your brain. [zh:想起来], on the other hand, is the action of successfully retrieving that memory from your brain at a specific moment. For example, you could say 'I remember that he took my class, but I can't recall his name right now.' In Chinese, that would be: [zh:我记得他上过我的课，可是我想不起来他叫什么名字]. So, [zh:记得] is the state, and [zh:想起来] is the action.

highlight_words.explanation_en: Refers to the continuous state of having a memory.
Refers to the mental act of retrieving a memory.

segments.teaching_goal: Learn the negative form 'xiǎng bu qǐlai' to say you can't remember.

narration_track.subtitle_en: Li You's response is [zh:对不起,我想不起来]. She starts with [zh:对不起], 'sorry'. Then she says [zh:我想不起来]. This is the negative form of the phrase we just learned. The structure 'Verb + bu + Complement' is used to negate potential complements. So, [zh:想起来] means 'to be able to recall', and [zh:想不起来] means 'to be unable to recall'. It's a polite way of saying 'I can't remember'.

content.focus_text: 对不起,我想不起来。
content.focus_pinyin: duìbuqǐ, wǒ xiǎng bu qǐlai.
content.focus_gloss_en: Sorry, I can't recall.

segments.visual_notes: Show the structure '想 + 不 + 起来' to emphasize the negative form.

segments.teaching_goal: Learn vocabulary for school connections.

narration_track.subtitle_en: Mr. Fei provides another piece of information. He says, [zh:我是高小音的中学同学]. This means, 'I am Gao Xiaoyin's middle school classmate.' The word [zh:中学] means 'middle school', and [zh:同学] means 'classmate'. This is a common way to establish a connection through a mutual friend.

content.focus_text: 我是高小音的中学同学。
content.focus_pinyin: wǒ shì Gāo Xiǎoyīn de zhōngxué tóngxué.
content.focus_gloss_en: I'm Gao Xiaoyin's middle school classmate.

segments.visual_notes: Show an icon of a school, then two stick figures labeled '同学'.

segments.teaching_goal: Learn to ask 'how' something happened using the 'shì...de' structure.

narration_track.subtitle_en: Li You is surprised. She says [zh:是吗], 'Really?'. Then she asks, [zh:你是怎么知道我的电话号码的]. This is a very useful sentence pattern: 'shì...de' is used to ask for details about a past event, in this case, 'how' he got her number. [zh:怎么] means 'how', [zh:知道] means 'to know', and [zh:电话号码] is 'phone number'. The question emphasizes the manner in which the action took place.

content.focus_text: 是吗?你是怎么知道我的电话号码的?
content.focus_pinyin: shì ma? nǐ shì zěnme zhīdào wǒ de diànhuà hàomǎ de?
content.focus_gloss_en: Really? How did you know my phone number?

content.notes: The '是...的' (shì...de) structure emphasizes details of a past action.

segments.visual_notes: Highlight the '是...的' structure around the main verb phrase.

segments.teaching_goal: Learn how to answer a 'shì...de' question.

narration_track.subtitle_en: Mr. Fei's answer uses the same 'shì...de' structure. He says, [zh:是小音告诉我的]. This means, 'It was Xiaoyin who told me.' The word [zh:告诉] means 'to tell'. By using this structure, he is directly answering the 'how' or 'who' aspect of her question, emphasizing that Xiaoyin was the source of the information.

content.focus_text: 是小音告诉我的。
content.focus_pinyin: shì Xiǎoyīn gàosu wǒ de.
content.focus_gloss_en: Xiaoyin told me.

segments.visual_notes: Show an arrow from 'Xiaoyin' to the phone number, illustrating the act of 'telling'.

segments.teaching_goal: Learn the phrase 'yǒu shì ma' to ask about the purpose of a call.

narration_track.subtitle_en: Li You gets to the point. She says, [zh:费先生,你有事吗]. She addresses him as [zh:费先生], 'Mr. Fei'. The question [zh:你有事吗] literally means 'Do you have business/matter?'. In this context, it's a way of asking 'Is there something you need?' or 'What's up?'. It can sound a bit direct, suggesting she wants to know the purpose of his call.

content.focus_text: 费先生,你有事吗?
content.focus_pinyin: Fèi xiānsheng, nǐ yǒu shì ma?
content.focus_gloss_en: Mr. Fei, is there something you need?

highlight_words.translation: to be occupied; to have something to do
to be occupied; to have something to do

segments.visual_notes: Show a speech bubble with a question mark inside, indicating 'what's the purpose?'.

segments.teaching_goal: Learn how to ask if someone is free and invite them out.

narration_track.subtitle_en: Here comes the invitation. Mr. Fei asks, [zh:这个周末你有空儿吗]. [zh:这个周末] is 'this weekend', and [zh:有空儿] means 'to have free time'. Then he states his intention: [zh:我想请你去跳舞]. [zh:想] means 'would like to', and [zh:请] means 'to invite'. So, he's saying, 'I'd like to invite you to go dancing.' This is a classic way to ask someone out.

content.focus_text: 这个周末你有空儿吗?我想请你去跳舞。
content.focus_pinyin: zhè ge zhōumò nǐ yǒu kòngr ma? wǒ xiǎng qǐng nǐ qù tiàowǔ.
content.focus_gloss_en: Are you free this weekend? I'd like to ask you to go dancing.

highlight_words.translation: to want to; would like to
to want to; would like to

segments.visual_notes: Show a calendar icon with the weekend highlighted. Then an icon of two people dancing.

segments.teaching_goal: Learn how to refuse an invitation and give a reason.

narration_track.subtitle_en: Here is the first refusal. Li You says, [zh:这个周末不行]. The word [zh:不行] means 'won't work' or 'not okay', and it's a common way to decline. She then gives a reason: [zh:下个星期我有三个考试]. [zh:下个星期] is 'next week', and [zh:考试] means 'test' or 'exam'. Having three exams is a very solid reason to be busy!

content.focus_text: 这个周末不行,下个星期我有三个考试。
content.focus_pinyin: zhè ge zhōumò bù xíng, xià ge xīngqī wǒ yǒu sān ge kǎoshì.
content.focus_gloss_en: This weekend won't work. I have three tests next week.

segments.visual_notes: Show a big red 'X' over the weekend on the calendar. Then show icons of three test papers.

segments.teaching_goal: Learn phrases for making an alternative suggestion.

narration_track.subtitle_en: Mr. Fei doesn't give up. He starts with [zh:没关系], 'no problem'. He then suggests an alternative: [zh:下个周末怎么样], 'How about next weekend?'. He adds an incentive: [zh:你考完试,我们好好儿玩儿玩儿]. [zh:考完试] means 'after you finish your tests'. The phrase [zh:好好儿玩儿玩儿] means 'we can have a really good time'. We'll look at this special expression next.

content.focus_text: 没关系,下个周末怎么样? ...我们好好儿玩儿玩儿。
content.focus_pinyin: méi guānxi, xià ge zhōumò zěnme yàng? ...wǒmen hǎohāor wánr wánr.
content.focus_gloss_en: No problem, how about next weekend? ...we can go have a good time.

segments.visual_notes: Show a checkmark on 'next weekend' on the calendar. Animate the words '好好儿' to sparkle.

segments.teaching_goal: Understand the colloquial expression 'hǎohāor' to mean 'all out' or 'to one's heart's content'.

narration_track.subtitle_en: Let's focus on the expression [zh:好好儿]. This is a colloquial phrase that means 'all out' or 'to one's heart's content'. It often comes before a verb to act as an adverbial, emphasizing that the action should be done thoroughly and enjoyably. For example, [zh:好好儿学习] means 'study hard', and [zh:好好儿休息] means 'get a good rest'. In the dialogue, [zh:好好儿玩儿玩儿] means 'to have a really great time'. Note the tone on the second 'hao' is neutral or light, not the third tone.

highlight_words.translation: all out; to one's heart's content
highlight_words.explanation_en: A colloquial expression used as an adverbial to emphasize doing an action thoroughly or with great enjoyment.

segments.teaching_goal: Learn vocabulary related to moving and cleaning.

narration_track.subtitle_en: Li You refuses again. She says, [zh:下个周末也不行], 'Next weekend also won't work.' Her reason this time is: [zh:我要从宿舍搬出去]. This means 'I have to move out of the dorm'. [zh:搬] is 'to move', and [zh:出去] means 'out'. She adds, [zh:得打扫、整理房间]. The word [zh:得] means 'must'. The two verbs are [zh:打扫], 'to clean', and [zh:整理], 'to tidy up' the [zh:房间], or 'room'.

content.focus_text: 下个周末也不行,我要从宿舍搬出去,得打扫、整理房间。
content.focus_pinyin: xià ge zhōumò yě bù xíng, wǒ yào cóng sùshè bān chūqù, děi dǎsǎo, zhěnglǐ fángjiān.
content.focus_gloss_en: Next weekend won't work either. I have to move out of the dorm, so I have to clean and tidy up the room.

segments.visual_notes: Show an icon of a person carrying a box out of a building. Then show icons for a broom (打扫) and a neat stack of books (整理).

segments.teaching_goal: Learn how to say 'the weekend after next' and a soft way to make a suggestion.

narration_track.subtitle_en: Amazingly, Mr. Fei tries a third time! He says, [zh:你看下下个周末,好不好]. The phrase [zh:下下个周末] means 'the weekend after next'. The structure [zh:你看..., 好不好] is a very soft and suggestive way to propose an idea. It's like saying, 'How about you consider..., is that okay?'. It's less direct than just asking a question.

content.focus_text: 你看下下个周末,好不好?
content.focus_pinyin: nǐ kàn xià xià ge zhōumò, hǎo bu hǎo?
content.focus_gloss_en: How about the weekend after next, is that okay?

segments.visual_notes: Show a calendar flipping two weeks ahead. Highlight the '好不好' structure.

segments.teaching_goal: Learn how to give a final, undeniable reason for refusal.

narration_track.subtitle_en: This is the final, decisive refusal. Li You says [zh:下下个周末更不行了]. The word [zh:更] means 'even more', so this means 'The weekend after next is even less possible!'. She delivers the final blow with her reason: [zh:我要跟我的男朋友去纽约旅行]. 'I'm going to travel to New York with my boyfriend.' This introduces [zh:男朋友] for 'boyfriend' and [zh:旅行] for 'to travel'. This is a very clear and unambiguous excuse.

content.focus_text: 对不起,下下个周末更不行了,我要跟我的男朋友去纽约旅行。
content.focus_pinyin: duìbuqǐ, xià xià ge zhōumò gèng bùxíng le, wǒ yào gēn wǒ de nánpéngyou qù Niǔyuē lǚxíng.
content.focus_gloss_en: Sorry, the weekend after next is even worse. I'm going to travel to New York with my boyfriend.

segments.visual_notes: Show an icon of a couple holding hands, next to a landmark for New York like the Statue of Liberty.

segments.teaching_goal: Recognize filler words that indicate hesitation.

narration_track.subtitle_en: After hearing about the boyfriend, Mr. Fei is speechless. He just says [zh:那]. In this context, with the pauses, [zh:那] is a filler word that shows hesitation or that he's at a loss for what to say next. It's like saying 'Oh... well...' in English. He has finally run out of suggestions.

content.focus_gloss_en: ...Oh... well...

segments.visual_notes: Show ellipsis points '...' growing on screen to represent the awkward pause.

segments.teaching_goal: Learn a common excuse to end a phone call abruptly.

narration_track.subtitle_en: Li You quickly ends the awkward conversation. She uses another excuse: [zh:我的手机没电了], 'My phone is out of battery.' This is a very common, if not always true, way to get off the phone quickly. She then says a quick [zh:再见], 'goodbye', and hangs up. This is a very clear signal that the conversation is over.

content.focus_text: 费先生,对不起,我的手机没电了。再见!
content.focus_pinyin: Fèi xiānsheng, duìbuqǐ, wǒ de shǒujī méi diàn le. zàijiàn!
content.focus_gloss_en: Mr. Fei, sorry, my phone is out of battery. Goodbye!

segments.visual_notes: Show a phone icon with a battery level that quickly drops to zero.

segments.teaching_goal: Understand the context of a final, unanswered 'wèi'.

narration_track.subtitle_en: The dialogue ends with Mr. Fei left on the line. He says [zh:喂] again, but this time it's not a greeting. He's trying to see if she's still there, like saying 'Hello? Hello?' into a dead line. The long pauses indicated by the ellipsis show his confusion and the abrupt end of the call. Poor Mr. Fei!

content.focus_pinyin: wèi... wèi...
content.focus_gloss_en: Hello...? Hello...?

segments.visual_notes: Show the word '喂' on screen, then have it fade out, followed by a 'call ended' screen.

segments.teaching_goal: To understand simple directional complements using verbs like 上, 下, 进, 出 combined with 来 or 去.

narration_track.subtitle_en: Let's dive into a key grammar point: directional complements. These show the direction of an action. A simple directional complement is formed by a verb plus a direction word like [zh:上], [zh:下], [zh:进], or [zh:出]. To specify direction relative to the speaker, we add [zh:来] for 'coming' or [zh:去] for 'going'. For example, in the dialogue, Li You says she has to [zh:搬出去], 'move out'. The action is [zh:搬], 'to move', and the direction is [zh:出去], 'out and away from' where she currently is. The choice between [zh:来] and [zh:去] depends entirely on the speaker's perspective.

content.focus_text: Verb + [上/下/进/出] + [来/去]
content.focus_gloss_en: Structure for Simple Directional Complements

grammar_points.pattern_pinyin: wǒ yào cóng sùshè bān chūqù
grammar_points.explanation_en: I need to move out of the dorm. (The speaker is inside, moving away).
She is coming downstairs. (The speaker is downstairs).
The teacher is walking into the classroom. (The speaker is outside the classroom).

content.notes: 来 (lái) indicates movement towards the speaker, while 去 (qù) indicates movement away.

grammar_points.pattern_pinyin: wǒ yào cóng sùshè bān chūqù
grammar_points.explanation_en: I need to move out of the dorm. (The speaker is inside, moving away).
She is coming downstairs. (The speaker is downstairs).
The teacher is walking into the classroom. (The speaker is outside the classroom).

segments.visual_notes: Animate an arrow moving away from a dorm for '搬出去', an arrow coming down stairs for '下楼来', and an arrow going into a classroom for '走进教室去'.

segments.teaching_goal: To understand compound and abstract uses of directional complements, like 想起来.

narration_track.subtitle_en: Directional complements can also be abstract. The dialogue gives us a fantastic example with [zh:想起来]. Here, [zh:想] means 'to think' and the complement [zh:起来] implies an upward or emerging action. Literally 'think up,' it means 'to recall' or 'to bring a memory to the surface.' When Li You says [zh:我想不起来], she uses the potential form by inserting [zh:不] between the verb and the complement. This shows the action of recalling is not achievable at that moment. This is a very common and useful pattern for expressing ability or inability.

content.focus_text: Q: 想起来了吗？ A: 我想不起来。
content.focus_pinyin: xiǎng qǐlai le ma? wǒ xiǎng bu qǐlai.
content.focus_gloss_en: Q: Do you remember now? A: I can't recall.

grammar_points.explanation_en: Affirmative: 'I recalled it.' The action was successfully completed.
Negative Potential: 'I cannot recall.' The action is not achievable.

grammar_points.pattern_pinyin: tā mǎi huí lai le yì xiē shuǐguǒ
grammar_points.explanation_en: Compound complement: He bought some fruit and brought it back here. (Action: buy; Direction: back here).

content.notes: The potential form: Verb + 得/不 + Complement

grammar_points.explanation_en: Affirmative: 'I recalled it.' The action was successfully completed.
Negative Potential: 'I cannot recall.' The action is not achievable.

grammar_points.pattern_pinyin: tā mǎi huí lai le yì xiē shuǐguǒ
grammar_points.explanation_en: Compound complement: He bought some fruit and brought it back here. (Action: buy; Direction: back here).

segments.visual_notes: Show a lightbulb icon appearing over a character's head for '想起来'. For '想不起来', show the lightbulb flickering and disappearing.

segments.segment_title: Usage: 记得 vs. 想起来
segments.teaching_goal: To distinguish between the state of remembering (记得) and the action of recalling (想起来).

narration_track.subtitle_en: Let's clarify the difference between two words for 'remember': [zh:记得] and [zh:想起来]. [zh:记得] describes the continuous state of having a memory. In the dialogue, Mr. Fei asks, [zh:你还记得我吗], 'Do you still remember me?' He's asking if the memory of him exists in her mind. On the other hand, [zh:想起来] is the action of successfully retrieving a memory. When Mr. Fei asks [zh:想起来了吗], he's asking, 'Have you managed to recall it now?' Li You's reply, [zh:我想不起来], means she is trying, but cannot complete the action of recalling. So, [zh:记得] is about the state, while [zh:想起来] is about the action.

highlight_words.explanation_en: Refers to the continuous state of having something in one's memory.
Refers to the action of retrieving a memory, often after some thought.

segments.visual_notes: Show two columns. Left column for '记得' with an icon of a brain with information stored. Right column for '想起来' with an icon of a file being retrieved from a cabinet.

segments.teaching_goal: To learn the colloquial adverb 好好儿, meaning 'thoroughly' or 'to one's heart's content'.

narration_track.subtitle_en: Now let's look at the colloquial expression [zh:好好儿]. When Mr. Fei says [zh:我们好好儿玩儿玩儿], he's suggesting they 'have a really good time' or 'enjoy themselves to the fullest.' [zh:好好儿] functions as an adverb, placed before a verb, to indicate that an action should be done thoroughly, properly, or with great enjoyment. Notice the tone on the second [zh:好] is neutral, so we say 'hǎohāor'. You can use it for many activities, like [zh:好好儿学习] 'study hard' or [zh:好好儿休息] 'get a good rest'.

content.focus_text: 我们好好儿玩儿玩儿。
content.focus_pinyin: wǒmen hǎohāor wánr wánr
content.focus_gloss_en: Let's go have a good time.

grammar_points.explanation_en: Structure: Use 'hǎohāor' before a verb to intensify it.
Example: You must prepare for the test thoroughly.

content.notes: 好好儿 (hǎohāor) often precedes a reduplicated verb to add emphasis.

highlight_words.translation: all out, to one's heart's content
highlight_words.explanation_en: A colloquial adverb used to express doing an action thoroughly or with great enjoyment.

grammar_points.explanation_en: Structure: Use 'hǎohāor' before a verb to intensify it.
Example: You must prepare for the test thoroughly.

segments.visual_notes: Animate the characters '好好儿' glowing before the verb '玩儿玩儿' to show its adverbial function.

segments.teaching_goal: To review the key vocabulary and grammar patterns for inviting and refusing invitations.

narration_track.subtitle_en: Great job! Let's quickly review the key points from this lesson. We learned how to use directional complements like [zh:搬出去] and [zh:想起来], and how to differentiate between [zh:记得] and [zh:想起来]. We also covered the useful expression [zh:好好儿] for doing something to the fullest. Keep practicing these phrases for inviting and refusing!

content.focus_text: Key Phrases Review
